campaign.title: Boltz-2 Inference Speedup
campaign.problem: How much can we speed up Boltz-2 GPU inference while keeping per-complex lDDT within 2 percentage points of the baseline?
campaign.metric_description: Speedup factor at iso-quality: T_baseline / T_optimized, subject to mean_pLDDT(optimized) >= mean_pLDDT(baseline) - 0.02

best.orbit: bypass-lightning (ODE-12/0r + TF32 + bf16 + bypass Trainer + CUDA warmup)

eval_methodology.summary: GPU-only predict timing (median of 3 seeds) on 3 test complexes. Baseline: 200-step EDM/Karras, 3 recycling steps.

references.note: Boltz-2: Towards Accurate and Efficient Binding Affinity Prediction
lDDT: local superposition-free score for comparing protein structures

timeline.text:
## Wave 1: Initial Exploration

Spawning 3 independent orbit agents to explore the highest-impact speedup axes:

| Orbit | Issue | Strategy | Expected Impact |
|-------|-------|----------|-----------------|
| step-reduction | #3 | Sweep diffusion steps 200→10, recycling 3→1 | 4-10x on diffusion stage |
| compile-tf32 | #4 | TF32 matmul + torch.compile | 1.3-2x steady state |
| flash-sdpa | #5 | Replace einsum attention with SDPA/FlashAttention-2 | 1.5-3x on attention |

**Baseline:** mean_wall_time = 70.37s, mean_pLDDT = 0.7107, on L40S
**Status:** All 3 agents spawning now.
timeline.author: liambai2

timeline.text:
**orbit/flash-sdpa complete**: metric=0.745x (**dead-end** — slower than baseline)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/flash-sdpa) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/flash-sdpa/orbits/flash-sdpa/log.md) · [figures](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/flash-sdpa/orbits/flash-sdpa/figures)

**Key insight:** SDPA replacement provides no speedup because Boltz's pair bias blocks FlashAttention-2. PyTorch falls back to math/mem_efficient backends which are not faster than einsum at short sequence lengths (200-600 tokens). However, **bf16 attention precision is safe** (pLDDT regression < 0.3pp) — useful for other optimization approaches.

Status: dead-end. No parent orbit.
timeline.author: liambai2

timeline.text:
**orbit/step-reduction complete**: metric=**1.73x speedup** (promising, current winner)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/step-reduction) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/step-reduction/orbits/step-reduction/log.md) · [figures](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/step-reduction/orbits/step-reduction/figures)

**Key insight:** Diffusion step count is NOT the bottleneck for end-to-end inference — MSA + trunk recycling dominate wall time. Reducing steps 200→20 alone gives only 0.95x. The real lever is `recycling_steps=0`, which cuts time from ~70s to ~41s. Best config: 20 steps, 0 recycles = **1.73x speedup, +1.56pp pLDDT** (quality actually improves).

Status: promising, winner. No parent orbit.
timeline.author: liambai2

timeline.text:
**orbit/compile-tf32 complete**: metric=0.97x (**dead-end** — no speedup)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/compile-tf32) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/compile-tf32/orbits/compile-tf32/log.md)

**Key insights:**
1. **TF32 is a no-op** — Boltz-2 already uses bf16-mixed precision, so TF32 (which only affects fp32 matmuls) has nothing to optimize.
2. **torch.compile hurts** (0.49x) — JIT compilation overhead of 80-120s per shape is never amortized in single-shot inference with varying input shapes.
3. `compile_pairformer` and `compile_msa` are dead code during inference (revert to uncompiled via `._orig_mod`).

Status: dead-end. No parent orbit.

timeline.text:
**reviewer:** Cross-Orbit Review — Milestone #1 (after 3 orbits) --- ### Search Tree ```mermaid graph TD main["main
baseline: 1.00x
70.37s, pLDDT 0.7107"] sr["step-reduction #3
1.73x — 40.7s, +1.56pp
20 steps, 0 recycles"] ct["compile-tf32 #4
0.97x — dead-end
TF32=no-op, compile=2x overhead"] fs["flash-sdpa #5
0.745x — dead-end
pair bias blocks FA2"] main --> sr main --> ct main --> fs style sr fill:#FBCA04,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style ct fill:#B60205,color:#fff style fs fill:#B60205,color:#fff ``` --- ### Leaderboard | Rank | Orbit | Strategy | Metric | Delta Baseline | Status | Links | |------|-------|----------|--------|---------------|--------|-------| | 1 | step-reduction | 20 steps + 0 recycles | **1.73x** | +1.56pp pLDDT | winner | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/step-reduction) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/step-reduction/orbits/step-reduction/log.md) | | 2 | compile-tf32 | TF32 + torch.compile | 0.97x | 0.00pp | dead-end | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/compile-tf32) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/compile-tf32/orbits/compile-tf32/log.md) | | 3 | flash-sdpa | SDPA / FlashAttention-2 | 0.745x | -0.12pp | dead-end | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/flash-sdpa) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/flash-sdpa/orbits/flash-sdpa/log.md) | Baseline: 1.00x, 70.37s mean, pLDDT 0.7107 (L40S) --- ### What Is Working - **Removing trunk recycling is the dominant speedup lever.** Reducing recycling_steps from 3 to 0 cuts mean wall time from ~70s to ~41s (1.7x). This is the strongest result in the campaign. The step-reduction orbit showed that 200→20 diffusion steps alone yields only 0.95x because the trunk runs recycling_steps+1 times and its cost swamps the diffusion loop in end-to-end timing. - **Low step counts are quality-safe down to 20 steps.** The step-reduction orbit confirmed the Pareto-safe zone: 20 steps at recycling_steps=0 passes the quality gate with a +1.56pp pLDDT improvement. The AlphaFold3 paper (Abramson et al. 2024) noted acceptable quality at 20-50 steps; this orbit confirms that finding holds for Boltz-2. - **bf16 attention is safe.** The flash-sdpa orbit found that casting attention ops from float32 to bf16 causes less than 0.3pp pLDDT regression. This opens the door for bf16 attention paths in future approaches without a quality penalty. --- ### What Is Not Working - **TF32 is a no-op for Boltz-2.** The problem statement expected ~10% free speedup from enabling TF32. The compile-tf32 orbit confirmed this is moot: Boltz-2 already uses bf16-mixed precision, so there are essentially no fp32 matmuls for TF32 to accelerate. Any future approach that assumes fp32 as a starting point has already been ruled out. - **torch.compile is counterproductive in single-shot inference.** The JIT overhead is 80-120 seconds per unique input shape. With the harness running one prediction per subprocess and three test complexes of different sizes, compile overhead is never amortized. torch.compile would only help in a persistent-model serving scenario where hundreds of same-shape predictions run in the same process. - **FlashAttention-2 (and SDPA generally) provides no speedup.** The root cause is architectural: Boltz's pair bias term (shape B×H×N×N) is added to every attention layer. FA2 does not support arbitrary additive masks, so PyTorch falls back to math/mem_efficient backends, which are not faster than einsum for short sequences (200-600 tokens). The SDPA path also requires a layout transpose (B,N,H,D) → (B,H,N,D) that adds overhead. See orbit/flash-sdpa. --- ### Key Insights 1. **The problem.md cost model was wrong.** The research document states "Stage 5 [diffusion, 200 steps] accounts for the large majority of wall-clock inference time." In end-to-end timing (including MSA server), this is not true. The trunk recycling (4 passes of MSA module + 48/64 Pairformer blocks) plus MSA server latency are co-dominant costs. The diffusion loop only dominates GPU-only time. This inversion changes the priority of what to optimize next. 2. **recycling_steps=0 may improve pLDDT via a sampling artifact.** Three runs showed +1.56pp mean improvement. This is counterintuitive — fewer recycling iterations should produce weaker representations. The orbit notes this may be a stochastic seed effect on 3 complexes and should not be over-interpreted. A proper 20-complex Tier 1 validation would clarify whether the improvement is real or noise. 3. **MSA server latency is a major noise source.** Run-to-run variance of 5-30 seconds makes speedup ratios uncertain at roughly ±10%. The 50-step, 1-recycle configuration showed a 107s outlier (vs. 43s expected). Pre-caching MSA results would dramatically reduce measurement variance and is a priority for any future orbit that needs precise attribution. 4. **compile_pairformer and compile_msa are dead code during inference.** The orbit/compile-tf32 agent found that these flags trigger `_orig_mod` revert logic at inference time, effectively un-compiling the modules after loading. This is a codebase bug that future agents should be aware of when considering kernel-level optimizations. --- ### Connections Between Orbits - orbit/step-reduction (recycling) and orbit/compile-tf32 (compile infrastructure) converge on the same insight: the trunk (Pairformer + MSA) is the binding bottleneck, not the diffusion loop. Step-reduction demonstrated this empirically; compile-tf32 confirmed it by showing that even fully compiled diffusion steps (if overhead were amortized) would not move the needle because the trunk is unaffected. - orbit/flash-sdpa's finding that bf16 attention precision is safe (< 0.3pp regression) is directly enabling for any future orbit that applies reduced-precision or fused-kernel approaches to Pairformer attention. The quality budget is not consumed by precision reduction. - All three orbits suffer from the same MSA server noise problem. This is a cross-cutting infrastructure issue, not a per-orbit quirk. --- ### Relevant Literature - [Abramson et al., Nature 2024](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07487-w) — AlphaFold3 reports acceptable quality at 20-50 diffusion steps; confirmed by step-reduction orbit for Boltz-2. - [Karras et al., NeurIPS 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.00364) — EDM-style samplers converge faster than DDPM, supporting the step reduction rationale. - [Dao et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08691) — FlashAttention-2 does not support additive bias masks; directly explains flash-sdpa's failure. - [Song et al., 2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.02502) — DDIM deterministic sampling; a natural next orbit given that stochastic noise injection in the current Boltz sampler may be unnecessary at inference time (gamma_0=0 → deterministic ODE). - [Passaro et al., bioRxiv 2025](https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.06.14.659707) — Boltz-2 paper; confirms bf16-mixed precision architecture. --- ### Figure Quality Audit **orbit/step-reduction — pareto_frontier.png** Panel (a) bar chart is clear and readable. Panel (b) Pareto scatter is the most information-dense panel and is the "Figure 1 candidate" for this campaign — it directly shows the quality-speed tradeoff across all tested configurations. Panel (c) line chart shows wall time vs step count and makes the recycling dominance visually obvious (the recycling=0 line is flat near 40s while recycling=3 stays near 70-80s regardless of step count). Issues found: - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/step-reduction pareto_frontier.png: Panel (a) and (c) font sizes are smaller than the style guide minimum. Labels on the x-axis in panel (a) ("10", "20", "50", "100", "200 Sampling steps") appear very small. The style guide requires font.size=11 minimum. Suggest: increase figure width or label font size on panel (a) and (c). These do not prevent interpretation but would be a problem in a publication PDF. - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/step-reduction pareto_frontier.png: Panel (b) uses point labels (e.g., "100s/0r", "50s/0r") with no explicit annotation arrows. Some labels likely overlap near the cluster of recycling=0 points at similar x-positions. Suggest: use `ax.annotate()` with offset xytext to fan out the labels. - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/step-reduction per_complex_breakdown.png: Panel (b) "Wall Time" bar chart uses blue tones that are visually close for Small/Medium/Large across the different configurations. The style guide specifies the muted discrete palette `['#4C72B0', '#DD8452', '#55A868']` for 3 discrete categories. The current bars look like a single-hue gradient, which makes it harder to distinguish complex size. **orbit/flash-sdpa — results.png** Panel (a) bar chart is well-structured. Panel (b) speedup bar chart includes a clear quality gate annotation (green dashed line) which is a good practice — readers immediately see which bars fail. Panel (c) pLDDT scatter (baseline vs SDPA bf16) is creative and clearly shows quality preservation, but the "medium" complex point falls at pLDDT ~0.46, which is conspicuously lower than the other two. This should be called out in the caption (it is in the log text but not the figure itself). Issues found: - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/flash-sdpa results.png: Panel (c) has no title. The style guide requires panel labels `(a)`, `(b)`, `(c)` and titles. Panel (c) is missing its title entirely, leaving it ambiguous without reading the surrounding prose. - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/flash-sdpa results.png: Panel (a) error bars appear to be extremely tight on the medium complex, which masks the known MSA server variance. If error bars represent std, this is likely because only one run had the anomalous latency. Suggest: plot individual points as jittered dots over the bar for n=3 data (the style guide recommends violin/box for distributions, but with n=3 a dot plot is the correct choice). **orbit/compile-tf32 — NO FIGURE** The compile-tf32 orbit has no figure at all. This is a gap. The log table is clear, but the style guide states "Every research problem has a natural visual representation." A simple 4-bar chart (baseline, TF32, TF32+compile_structure, TF32+compile_confidence) with speedup on y-axis would take 10 lines of code and would make the story immediately visible. - [FIGURE ISSUE] orbit/compile-tf32: No figure produced. The orbit log contains only tables. Add a single-panel bar chart showing speedup vs. configuration. **Cross-orbit comparability:** - [CROSS-ORBIT FIGURE] All three orbits plot speedup on different y-axis ranges. step-reduction uses 0.6-1.8x (auto), flash-sdpa uses 0-1.2x (auto), compile-tf32 has no figure. If a reader wants to compare across orbits visually, they must rescale mentally. Suggest: establish a shared y-axis range (0.0-2.0x) as a canonical campaign standard and note it in research/style.md. - [CROSS-ORBIT FIGURE] The "baseline" bar appears independently in each orbit's figure (always set to 1.00x or to the raw 70.37s). There is no canonical "campaign baseline" figure in research/figures/. Suggest: create research/figures/baseline_summary.png showing per-complex timing breakdown once, and reference it from all orbit figures rather than re-plotting. --- ### Prose & Clarity Audit **orbit/step-reduction:** - Log opens with a Glossary and immediately states the result in the first line of the Results section. Feynman-test: PASS. Punchline is on line 1 of Results. - "Phase 1" and "Phase 2" structure is clear. The causal logic (step reduction alone fails → recycling is the lever → validate the best configs) is easy to follow. - [READABILITY] orbit/step-reduction: The "Limitations and Caveats" section notes that "Speedup metric has ~10% uncertainty from MSA latency variance" but does not state what this means for the 1.73x result concretely (is the true range 1.56x-1.91x?). Suggest: add a one-sentence confidence interval to the result statement, e.g., "1.73x ± 0.17x (estimated from MSA variance)." - The log is 500+ words as warned by the style guide. The tables are load-bearing, so the length is justified, but the "Approach" section repeats information already in Results. Suggest: trim the Phase 1/Phase 2 narration in Approach to cross-references only. **orbit/compile-tf32:** - Log opens with a clear result statement. Feynman-test: PASS. - The explanation of why torch.compile hurts (three enumerated sub-reasons) is precise and useful. A reader outside the subfield could follow it. - [READABILITY] orbit/compile-tf32: The "Wrapper Fix" subsection mentions monkey-patching `load_from_checkpoint` but does not explain what would happen without the fix. A reader might wonder: "was the compile flag actually being tested at all before the fix?" Add a sentence: "Without this fix, the compile flag had no effect — the baseline and 'compiled' configs were identical. All compile results in this log reflect the fixed wrapper." - [READABILITY] orbit/compile-tf32: No figure. Add at minimum a 4-bar speedup chart per the figure audit note above. **orbit/flash-sdpa:** - Log opens with a Glossary and clear result in the first line of Results. Feynman-test: PASS. - The "Why SDPA Does Not Help Here" section with 4 enumerated reasons is the clearest mechanistic explanation across all three orbits. Exemplary. - [READABILITY] orbit/flash-sdpa: The float32 SDPA mean speedup is reported as 0.55x but the orbit frontmatter `metric: 0.745` refers to bf16 SDPA. The difference is never reconciled in a single place. Suggest: add a "Which result is the headline?" note that explicitly says "We report bf16 SDPA as the primary result because it has lower variance (3 seeds all consistent), and float32 results were contaminated by MSA server latency on seed 7." - [READABILITY] orbit/flash-sdpa: The per-complex breakdown table (seed 7) notes "likely due to MSA server latency variance" for timing differences. The medium complex shows 71.2s vs 70.5s baseline — nearly identical — but large shows 104.6s vs 87.6s. The prose says the difference "is likely due to MSA server latency variance" but does not reconcile why the large complex specifically would have more variance. This leaves a loose end. Suggest: note that large complex has more tokens and thus more MSA computation, making it more sensitive to server queue latency. --- ### Novelty Audit All three orbits make no novel claims — they each include a "Prior Art & Novelty" section with an "Honest positioning" paragraph that correctly describes the work as empirical characterization, not discovery. **orbit/step-reduction:** Claims "discovery that recycling_steps dominates over diffusion steps in end-to-end timing." This is presented as evaluation-setup-specific and explicitly hedged. No overclaim. The AlphaFold3 parallel (20-50 steps are acceptable) is correctly cited and not claimed as new. **orbit/compile-tf32:** Claims "concrete measurement showing TF32 is a no-op for Boltz-2." Appropriately scoped. The discovery that compile_pairformer/compile_msa are dead code during inference is a codebase-specific finding; no prior art needed. **orbit/flash-sdpa:** Claims "empirical confirmation that SDPA replacement provides no speedup" and "validation that bf16 attention preserves quality." The log correctly notes that the FA2 mask limitation is already documented (Dao et al. 2023). No overclaim. All three orbits have "Prior Art & Novelty" sections. No [MISSING PRIOR ART] flags. No [NOVELTY WARNING] or [NOVELTY CAUTION] flags. The orbits are honest about being engineering characterizations. --- ### Reference Audit The shared registry at `research/references/registry.yaml` is empty (`references: []`). All three orbits cite papers inline in their logs but none have contributed to the registry. **Master Reference List** Papers cited across all orbits (from inline citations): | Paper | Cited by | Key finding | |-------|----------|-------------| | Abramson et al. 2024 (AlphaFold3) | step-reduction | Acceptable quality at 20-50 diffusion steps | | Karras et al. 2022 (EDM) | step-reduction | EDM samplers converge in fewer steps than DDPM | | Wohlwend et al. 2024 (Boltz-1) | step-reduction, flash-sdpa | Boltz architecture baseline | | Passaro et al. 2025 (Boltz-2) | compile-tf32, flash-sdpa | bf16-mixed precision, model architecture | | Dao et al. 2023 (FlashAttention-2) | flash-sdpa | FA2 does not support additive bias masks | | Jumper et al. 2021 (AlphaFold) | flash-sdpa | Pair bias attention architecture | | PyTorch SDPA docs | flash-sdpa, compile-tf32 | Backend dispatch logic | **Missing from registry:** All of the above (registry is empty). - [REFERENCE AUDIT] registry.yaml: No orbits have populated the shared reference registry. All citations live only in log.md inline text. This means the registry provides no deduplication or cross-orbit linkage. Suggest: any orbit agent that cites a paper should add it to the registry in the same commit. At minimum, the 7 papers above should be added. - [REFERENCE AUDIT] Missing potentially relevant references not yet in any orbit: (1) Song et al. 2020 DDIM — directly relevant to the deterministic ODE sampler direction; (2) Triton/OpenAI (Tillet et al. 2019) — relevant if Triton kernels are pursued for triangular attention; (3) Frantar et al. 2022 GPTQ — relevant for INT8/FP8 quantization orbit. --- ### Publication Readiness & Tier Upgrade **Core contribution (current):** Recycling_steps=0 + 20 diffusion steps yields 1.73x speedup at iso-quality for Boltz-2 on L40S, with pLDDT that matches or improves baseline. Three negative results (TF32, torch.compile, FlashAttention-2 via SDPA) close off the most-expected optimization paths. **Figure 1 candidate:** orbit/step-reduction pareto_frontier.png panel (b) — the quality-speed Pareto scatter across all recycling/step configurations. Directly answers the research question visually. **Publication readiness:** Not yet. The current test set has only 3 complexes. A credible paper requires: (1) Tier 1 validation on the CASP15 set to confirm generalizability; (2) understanding of why recycling_steps=0 improves pLDDT (currently unexplained); (3) stacking the best approaches (e.g., combine step reduction + any future kernel speedups) to push toward the 2-3x aspirational target; (4) addressing MSA latency noise with pre-cached MSA for controlled GPU-only timing. **Tier 2 upgrade candidates:** ``` [UPGRADE TO TIER 2] orbit/step-reduction: Core result is strong but rests on only 3 complexes and has unexplained pLDDT improvement at recycling_steps=0. Specific improvements needed: - [ ] Run on Tier 1 CASP15 set (20+ complexes) to confirm 1.73x holds and pLDDT improvement is not a 3-complex artifact - [ ] Characterize GPU-only timing (exclude MSA server) to cleanly attribute speedup to trunk vs. diffusion - [ ] Add confidence interval to the 1.73x headline (MSA variance makes ±10% uncertainty real) - [ ] Investigate why recycling_steps=0 improves pLDDT — is it stochastic sampling variance, or does single-pass trunk generalize better? ``` --- ### Graduation Candidates | Orbit | Status | Metric | Ready? | |-------|--------|--------|--------| | orbit/step-reduction | promising + winner | 1.73x | Not yet — needs Tier 1 validation and READABILITY cleanup | | orbit/compile-tf32 | dead-end | 0.97x | Not yet — no figure produced, READABILITY flags outstanding | | orbit/flash-sdpa | dead-end | 0.745x | Not yet — READABILITY flags outstanding, figure panel (c) missing title | No orbits are ready for graduation yet. The step-reduction orbit is the closest candidate pending Tier 1 validation. --- ### Narrative Arc **Thesis:** The primary inference bottleneck for Boltz-2 end-to-end latency is not the diffusion sampling loop (as the problem statement assumed) but the trunk computation (MSA + Pairformer recycling). The most impactful inference-time optimization is therefore recycling reduction, not step reduction or attention kernel replacement. **Key evidence:** - orbit/step-reduction (1.73x): Reducing diffusion steps 200→20 without changing recycling yields 0.95x (no benefit). Reducing recycling 3→0 with any step count yields 1.6-1.73x. The speedup is explained by trunk cost, not diffusion cost. - orbit/compile-tf32 (0.97x, dead-end): Ruled out precision and compile optimizations for the diffusion loop. - orbit/flash-sdpa (0.745x, dead-end): Ruled out attention kernel replacement. Pair bias is an architectural barrier to FlashAttention-2. **Open questions:** - Is the 1.73x result stable on a larger test set? Or is it inflated by MSA server noise on 3 complexes? - Why does recycling_steps=0 appear to improve pLDDT? Is the trunk representation at pass-1 already sufficient, or is this stochastic noise? - What is the GPU-only speedup from step reduction (excluding MSA)? The problem.md suggestion that diffusion dominates GPU-only time may still be correct — we just haven't isolated it. - Can we stack speedups? Step reduction + recycling reduction is already counted in the 1.73x. Adding a GPU-kernel speedup on the trunk (Triton triangular attention? fused LayerNorm?) could push beyond 2x. - Is there a smarter sampler (DDIM, consistency model) that converges in fewer steps than the current stochastic Karras sampler? **Story arc (if writing a paper today):** 1. Introduction: Boltz-2 is fast enough to predict structures but too slow for campaign-scale binder design. We characterize the true bottleneck. 2. Method: Systematic sweep of recycling steps and diffusion step count, plus ablation of compile/precision/attention kernel optimizations. 3. Results: orbit/step-reduction is the central experiment. Figures: Pareto scatter, per-complex breakdown. 4. Analysis: orbits/compile-tf32 and flash-sdpa as negative controls — ruling out the "obvious" approaches. Key finding: trunk recycling, not diffusion, is the bottleneck. 5. Missing: Tier 1 CASP15 validation; GPU-only timing breakdown; stacked optimizations toward 2-3x. **Shift from initial hypothesis:** The problem statement assumed diffusion dominates. The data shows trunk recycling is equally or more important for end-to-end latency. The campaign's next phase should focus on trunk-level optimizations (early-exit recycling, fused Triton kernels for triangular attention, deterministic ODE sampling at even lower step counts) rather than continuing to attack the diffusion sampling loop in isolation. --- ### Recommendations **Next 2-3 hypotheses:** 1. **orbit/ode-sampler** — Deterministic ODE sampling (gamma_0=0, noise_scale=1.0). The current stochastic Karras sampler injects noise at every step; a deterministic first-order ODE solver may converge in fewer steps. If 10 deterministic steps match 20 stochastic steps in quality, we get another 2x on the diffusion component. This is the cleanest untested high-priority item from the search space. Reference: Song et al. 2020 DDIM; Karras et al. 2022 EDM parameter analysis. 2. **orbit/cached-msa-timing** — Pre-cache MSA results and re-run the step-reduction winner (20 steps, 0 recycles) to get clean GPU-only timing. This is not a new optimization — it is a validation pass. Current speedup measurements are contaminated by 5-30s MSA server noise. A GPU-only baseline would (a) confirm the true speedup attribution, (b) reduce ±10% uncertainty, and (c) determine whether diffusion steps actually matter for GPU-time once MSA is excluded. This should be a light-weight REFINE, not a full orbit. 3. **orbit/early-exit-recycling** — Adaptive recycling termination: compute cosine similarity between pair representations at successive recycling passes; exit early when convergence is detected. This could reduce the effective recycling count below 3 for "easy" complexes while preserving quality on hard ones. Extends the step-reduction insight that recycling_steps=0 is viable for at least some structures. **Should we continue, pivot, or stop?** Continue. The campaign has not yet reached the 2-3x aspirational target, the dominant cost source (trunk recycling) has been identified but not fully exploited, and the most promising untested directions (ODE sampler, adaptive recycling, Triton kernels for triangular attention) are all within-scope no-retraining approaches. --- ### Action Mix ``` REFINE orbit/step-reduction: Re-run the 20-step/0-recycle winner with pre-cached MSA to isolate GPU-only timing. Also run 3 additional seeds to tighten the confidence interval on the 1.73x result. Append results to existing log. EXPLORE orbit/ode-sampler: Test gamma_0=0, noise_scale=1.0 (deterministic ODE) at 20, 10, and 5 diffusion steps. Compare quality against stochastic baseline at the same step counts. If 10 deterministic steps match 20 stochastic steps, the stacked speedup vs. baseline becomes ~2.5x (recycling=0 + ODE steps). EXPLORE orbit/early-exit-recycling: Implement cosine-similarity convergence criterion on pair representations; exit recycling early when delta < threshold. Sweep threshold values. Target: adaptive 1-2 passes for easy complexes, 3 for hard ones. CONCLUDE orbit/compile-tf32: TF32 is definitively a no-op; compile hurts in single-shot inference. No residual juice. Mark concluded. CONCLUDE orbit/flash-sdpa: FA2 cannot be used with pair bias without retraining. The only open extension would be FlashAttention-3 (which may support additive bias) — but that requires a library dependency not in the current tree. Mark concluded for now; re-open if FA3 support lands. ``` Search balance: explore=2, refine=1, concluded=2 (explore ratio: ~43%, within the recommended <70% ceiling)

timeline.text:
## Human Steering (Milestone 1)

**Accepted:** EXPLORE orbit/ode-sampler, EXPLORE orbit/early-exit-recycling

timeline.text:
**orbit/combined-fast complete**: metric=1.66x (dead-end — confirms parent's 1.73x, no additional config-space speedup found)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/combined-fast) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/combined-fast/orbits/combined-fast/log.md)

**Key findings:**
- Diffusion loop at 20 steps costs only 3-5s (7-12% of total) — further step reduction negligible
- 12 steps is a hard quality cliff (pLDDT 0.73→0.51)
- MSA server latency is dominant variance source (37-107s same config)
- **All config-space optimizations exhausted** — 2x requires architectural changes:
  1. MSA caching (eliminate 5-30s server latency)
  2. Persistent model serving (eliminate 3-5s model loading)
  3. Re-enable cuequivariance CUDA kernels (10-30% trunk speedup)
  4. Bundle complexes in single predict call

Practical ceiling with current evaluator: ~1.7-1.85x.

timeline.text:
**orbit/ode-sampler complete**: metric=**1.79x speedup** (NEW WINNER, beats step-reduction's 1.73x)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/ode-sampler) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/ode-sampler/orbits/ode-sampler/log.md) · [figures](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/ode-sampler/orbits/ode-sampler/figures)

**Key findings:**
1. **ODE at 10 steps preserves quality (pLDDT 0.73) where SDE collapses (0.41)** — deterministic ODE extends usable step range by 2x
2. **ODE-20 + recycling=0 = 1.79x** (vs SDE-20 at 1.67x same conditions) — modest but consistent improvement from eliminating per-step noise
3. ODE-10 quality is excellent but timing advantage masked by MSA variance — with pre-cached MSA could reach ~2.5x
4. gamma_0=0 monkey-patch is sufficient, no retraining needed

Parent: #3 (step-reduction). Builds on recycling=0 insight.

timeline.text:
**orbit/early-exit-recycling complete**: metric=0.95x (**dead-end** — strictly dominated by recycling_steps=0)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/early-exit-recycling) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/early-exit-recycling/orbits/early-exit-recycling/log.md)

**Key finding:** Early-exit recycling requires minimum 2 trunk passes (to compute convergence). recycling_steps=0 uses 1 pass. The structural argument is definitive: adaptive recycling **cannot** beat recycling=0 because its minimum cost is strictly higher. Convergence profile shows z similarity reaches 0.97-0.98 after pass 1 — high but not enough to exit before pass 2.

Useful negative result confirming recycling_steps=0 is optimal for this test set. Parent: #3 (step-reduction).
timeline.author: liambai2

timeline.text:
**orbit/l40s-kernels complete**: metric=1.71x (dead-end — GPU opts exist but masked by MSA latency)

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/l40s-kernels) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/l40s-kernels/orbits/l40s-kernels/log.md)

**Key findings:**
1. **cuequivariance kernels BLOCKED** — cublas version conflict (torch 2.5.1 pins cublas 12.4.5, cuequivariance needs ≥12.5). Requires torch 2.6+ upgrade.
2. **torch.compile deliberately disabled for inference** — Boltz reverts to uncompiled via `_orig_mod` due to recompilation overhead on variable-length inputs.
3. **TF32 gives 19% speedup on triangular multiply** — real but invisible in e2e metric due to MSA noise.
4. **bf16 triangular multiply shows 1.94x potential** — but Boltz explicitly upcasts to fp32 before einsum.
5. **GPU compute is ~25s of 41s total** — even 50% GPU speedup only gets e2e to ~28s (2.5x), but blocked by cuequivariance conflict.

**The evaluation metric's Achilles heel is MSA latency.** GPU optimizations are real but unmeasurable without pre-cached MSA or GPU-only timing.

+1.56pp pLDDT | [#9](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/issues/9) | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/combined-fast) | [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/combined-fast/orbits/combined-fast/log.md) | | 6 | compile-tf32 | TF32 only | **0.97x** | 0.00pp pLDDT | [#4](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/issues/4) | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/compile-tf32) | [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/compile-tf32/orbits/compile-tf32/log.md) | | 7 | flash-sdpa | bf16 SDPA (no FA2) | **0.745x** | -0.20pp pLDDT | [#5](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/issues/5) | [code](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/flash-sdpa) | [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/flash-sdpa/orbits/flash-sdpa/log.md) | --- ### What's Working **Recycling elimination is the single most powerful inference-time lever.** All four orbits that achieved speedup above 1.6x share one common configuration: `recycling_steps=0`. Reducing from 3 to 0 recycling iterations eliminates 3 of 4 Pairformer trunk passes, cutting ~30s from a ~70s baseline. This insight, first discovered in orbit/step-reduction, has been reproduced four times (step-reduction, ode-sampler, l40s-kernels, combined-fast) with consistent results in the 1.66–1.73x range. **Deterministic ODE sampling adds a modest incremental gain on top of recycling elimination.** Setting `gamma_0=0` converts the stochastic Karras sampler to a deterministic Euler ODE, yielding 1.79x vs 1.67x for SDE at the same 20-step/0-recycle configuration — a 7% relative improvement in wall time (39.3s vs 42.2s). The per-complex breakdown shows ODE saves 2–5s per complex, with the savings coming from eliminating per-step noise generation. More importantly, ODE maintains quality at 10 steps (pLDDT 0.73) where SDE collapses (pLDDT 0.41), extending the viable step-count range. **Quality is consistently better at 0 recycling than at baseline.** All configurations with `recycling_steps=0` show positive pLDDT delta (mean +1.56 to +1.96pp). While this is surprising and the 3-complex test set is too small to establish this as a general result, it is consistent across four independent orbit runs. --- ### What's Not Working **Diffusion-loop optimizations (without recycling reduction) yield no speedup.** Reducing steps from 200 to 20 while keeping `recycling_steps=3` gives 0.95x — within noise of baseline. The diffusion loop at 20 steps costs only ~3–5s out of ~41s total, making further step reduction negligible in end-to-end timing. **TF32 precision is a no-op for Boltz-2.** The model runs in bf16-mixed mode by default, leaving essentially no fp32 matmuls for TF32 to accelerate. The 0.97x result (orbit/compile-tf32) confirms this definitively. **torch.compile is harmful in the single-shot subprocess evaluation architecture.** Each complex runs in a fresh subprocess, so JIT compilation (80–120s overhead per shape) is never amortized. The inductor generates kernels inferior to the existing cuBLAS bf16 path for Boltz's complex attention patterns. This is a design decision by the Boltz authors: `boltz2.py:478-481` explicitly reverts compiled modules to `._orig_mod` at inference time. **FlashAttention-2 is architecturally blocked by pair bias.** Boltz adds a per-layer pair bias tensor (B, H, N_q, N_k) to every attention logit, which FA2 does not support as an arbitrary additive mask. Without pair bias, SDPA would need a full pair representation re-architecture — requiring retraining. Additionally, Boltz sequences (200–600 residues) are too short for FA2's memory-efficiency advantage to matter (FA2 gains mainly at N > 2K). **Early-exit recycling is structurally dominated by `recycling_steps=0`.** Convergence detection requires at least 2 trunk passes (one to generate z, one to check delta-z). This is a hard minimum cost that always exceeds the 1-trunk-pass cost of `recycling_steps=0`. orbit/early-exit-recycling established this with clean quantitative convergence profiles: cosine similarity after pass 1 is 0.97–0.98, reaching 0.99+ only by pass 3. **The config-space ceiling is ~1.7–1.85x.** Three independent orbits (combined-fast, l40s-kernels, ode-sampler all at their baseline reproduction point) converge on the same practical ceiling. The fixed overhead — MSA server latency (5–30s), model loading, preprocessing, single trunk pass — accounts for ~35–45s of the optimized 39–42s runtime. Config-only approaches cannot eliminate this overhead. --- ### Key Cross-Orbit Insights **The MSA latency theme dominates all timing comparisons.** MSA server round-trips add 5–30s of non-deterministic variance per complex per run. This has three consequences: (1) single-run comparisons are unreliable (orbit/combined-fast's single-run TF32 measurement showed 0.49x, a ~3x underestimate due to MSA cache miss); (2) GPU-level improvements worth 2–5s are invisible against 30s variance; (3) ODE-10 appeared slower than ODE-20 in end-to-end measurement despite having fewer steps. The evaluation methodology (median of 3 runs per complex) mitigates but does not eliminate this problem for small speedup increments. **The problem.md assumption that diffusion dominates was incorrect for end-to-end latency.** The document states "Stage 5 [diffusion, 200 steps] accounts for the large majority of wall-clock inference time." This is true for GPU-only timing, but MSA + trunk recycling + model loading dominate end-to-end time. orbit/combined-fast's profiling shows diffusion at 20 steps costs 3–5s out of ~41s total (7–12%). This means the initial search space prioritization was wrong: recycling reduction (search space item #6) was more impactful than step reduction (#1) or attention kernel replacement (#3). **bf16 precision is uniformly safe.** orbit/flash-sdpa validated that running attention in bf16 (rather than fp32) causes less than 0.3pp pLDDT regression. orbit/l40s-kernels confirmed that bf16 triangular multiply achieves 1.94x speedup on isolated ops vs fp32. The bottleneck to exploiting this is a single `x.float()` upcast in `triangular_mult.py:116`. If this upcast is removed and quality is preserved, bf16 throughout the trunk becomes a viable speedup. **The path to 2x+ is architectural, not configurational.** orbit/combined-fast and orbit/l40s-kernels both identified the same three unblocked paths: (1) MSA pre-caching to eliminate 5–30s variance and the non-GPU-acceleratable server latency, (2) upgrading torch to 2.6+ to unlock cuequivariance custom CUDA kernels (blocked by a cublas 12.4 vs 12.5 version conflict), (3) removing the fp32 upcast in triangular multiply. A fourth path — persistent model serving — would eliminate per-complex model loading overhead (~5–10s per cold process). --- ### Figure Quality Audit Figures exist in: orbit/step-reduction (`pareto_frontier.png`, `per_complex_breakdown.png`), orbit/ode-sampler (`ode_vs_sde_comparison.png`), orbit/flash-sdpa (`results.png`), orbit/l40s-kernels (`l40s_kernel_analysis.png`), orbit/early-exit-recycling (`convergence_analysis.png`), orbit/combined-fast (`analysis.png`). **Cross-orbit comparability issues:** [CROSS-ORBIT FIGURE] Orbits step-reduction, ode-sampler, early-exit-recycling, and combined-fast all plot speedup vs configuration, but the x-axis conventions differ across logs (step count vs config label vs threshold value). A reader cannot visually compare the Pareto frontier across orbits without reading each log independently. Suggest: promote a single master Pareto frontier figure to `research/figures/master_pareto.png` that overlays all configurations from all orbits on one axes. [CROSS-ORBIT FIGURE] The baseline bar (70.37s, pLDDT 0.7107) is regenerated as a reference in at least 4 orbit figures (step-reduction, ode-sampler, l40s-kernels, combined-fast). There should be one canonical baseline figure in `research/figures/`. This also means any run-to-run variance in the baseline bar draws attention away from the optimization being shown. [CROSS-ORBIT FIGURE] orbit/l40s-kernels contains a GPU op-level profiling figure (`l40s_kernel_analysis.png`) covering triangular multiply precision vs speed, while orbit/flash-sdpa covers attention SDPA vs einsum. These two figures together tell the story "which Boltz operations can be sped up at the kernel level" but are on separate branches with incompatible y-axis scales. They should be combined or at minimum cross-referenced. **Missing figure category:** There is no paper-ready "Figure 1" candidate that answers the research question at a glance. The closest candidate is orbit/step-reduction's `pareto_frontier.png` (speedup vs quality tradeoff), but it only covers the SDE branch and predates the ODE-sampler result. A new figure showing the full quality-speed Pareto frontier including ODE-20, SDE-20, and the baseline, with the 2pp quality gate shown as a horizontal line, would be the natural hook for a methods paper. --- ### Prose & Clarity Audit All 7 orbits use a consistent glossary format and define key terms (pLDDT, pp, MSA, etc.) on first use. The overall readability is high. Specific findings: [READABILITY] orbit/combined-fast: The `## Results` section lists the 1.66x result, but the punchline — "we have exhausted the config-space and confirmed the ceiling is 1.7–1.85x" — is buried in `## What I Learned` item 5 and `## Approach` subsection "Optimization search space exhaustion." A reader scanning the first 3 lines of Results would see a number but miss the strategic conclusion. Suggest: add a bold summary sentence at the top of Results: "This orbit confirms that no config-only path exists beyond ~1.8x with the current evaluator architecture." [READABILITY] orbit/l40s-kernels: The `## Results` section correctly reports 1.71x but the introductory sentence says "same config as step-reduction orbit, reproduced here." This is accurate but makes it sound like the orbit contributed nothing new. The actual contributions (cuequivariance diagnosis, TF32 profiling, bf16 upcast finding) are real and valuable. Suggest: restructure Results to lead with "This orbit did not achieve a new speedup record, but identified three specific blockers and unblocked paths..." rather than burying the diagnostic value. [READABILITY] orbit/early-exit-recycling: The convergence profile table (cosine similarity by pass and complex) is excellent and should be the first exhibit in Results, since it's the novel measurement. Instead it appears after the summary table. The structural argument for why early-exit cannot beat recycling=0 (the "2 trunk pass minimum" argument) is clear and rigorous — this is the punchline and it delivers well. [READABILITY] orbit/flash-sdpa: The explanation of why FA2 is blocked (pair bias, sequence length, tensor layout) is the best-written technical section across all 7 orbits. Good model for the campaign. --- ### Novelty Audit **orbit/step-reduction:** Claims "discovery that recycling steps (trunk computation), not diffusion steps, are the binding constraint for end-to-end speedup." This is specific to the evaluation setup (MSA server included in timing) and is stated with appropriate hedging. No novelty overclaim. **orbit/ode-sampler:** Claims ODE at 10 steps maintains Boltz-2 quality where SDE collapses. This is consistent with DDIM (Song et al. 2020) and EDM (Karras et al. 2022). The orbit correctly frames this as "empirical validation for Boltz-2" rather than a new theoretical result. [MISSING PRIOR ART] orbit/compile-tf32: No `## Prior Art & Novelty` section... actually, on re-reading, the section is present. However, it does not cite the AlphaFold3 inference optimization work. The Boltz-2 paper (Passaro et al. 2025) discusses precision choices; this should be cross-referenced explicitly. **orbit/flash-sdpa:** Correctly cites that FA2 does not support arbitrary additive attention masks (Dao et al. 2023). No overclaims. **orbit/l40s-kernels:** The cuequivariance cublas version conflict diagnosis is specific to the torch 2.5.1 + cuequivariance-ops-cu12 combination and is presented as a compatibility investigation, not a novel result. Appropriate. **orbit/early-exit-recycling:** Correctly notes that early stopping in iterative refinement is a known technique (AlphaFold2 uses it during training). The novel contribution — quantitative convergence profiles for Boltz-2 specifically — is clearly scoped. **orbit/combined-fast:** No novelty claims. Explicitly describes itself as "a thorough but ultimately negative result." Appropriate. Overall novelty posture: no false novelty claims across the campaign. The orbits are appropriately humble about applying known techniques to a new system. --- ### Reference Audit Papers cited across multiple orbits (foundational to this campaign): | Paper | Cited By | Key Relevance | |-------|----------|--------------| | Karras et al. NeurIPS 2022 (EDM) | step-reduction, ode-sampler, combined-fast | Sampler framework; gamma_0=0 ODE derivation | | Abramson et al. Nature 2024 (AF3) | step-reduction, early-exit-recycling, combined-fast | 20-50 step quality precedent; recycling design choices | | Wohlwend et al. bioRxiv 2024 (Boltz-1) | step-reduction, flash-sdpa, combined-fast | Architecture reference | | Passaro et al. bioRxiv 2025 (Boltz-2) | compile-tf32, l40s-kernels | bf16-mixed precision; architecture | | Dao et al. 2023 (FA2) | flash-sdpa | Pair bias mask limitation | | Song et al. ICLR 2021 (DDIM) | ode-sampler | Deterministic sampling foundation | | Jumper et al. Nature 2021 (AlphaFold2) | flash-sdpa, early-exit-recycling | Pair bias architecture; recycling design | **Missing from registry but potentially relevant:** - Mirdita et al. Nature Methods 2022 (ColabFold) — cited only in combined-fast; should be in the shared registry as it directly explains MSA server latency behavior - Triton (Tillet et al. 2019) — not yet cited; if any orbit pursues custom kernels, this will be the implementation framework - xFormers library (Meta 2022) — not cited; relevant to the mem_efficient SDPA backend used in flash-sdpa --- ### Publication Readiness **Core contribution (one sentence):** Reducing trunk recycling steps to zero, combined with deterministic ODE sampling at 20 steps, yields 1.79x end-to-end speedup for Boltz-2 inference with +1.96pp quality improvement on L40S hardware, at zero quality cost and no retraining. **Figure 1 candidate:** A quality-speed Pareto scatter (speedup x-axis, pLDDT y-axis) with one point per orbit/configuration, the 2pp gate shown as a dashed horizontal line, and the ODE-20/0r point highlighted as the Pareto frontier. This does not yet exist as a single figure but the data is complete. **Narrative arc:** Step reduction (orbit #1) → baseline characterization plus recycling discovery; ODE sampler (orbit #4) → deterministic sampling improves convergence and marginally improves speed; dead-end orbits (#2, #3, #5, #6, #7) as ablations ruling out every other approach. **Missing for completeness:** - Tier 1 (CASP15) validation of the 1.79x result on a larger, held-out test set. The current 3-complex result has high uncertainty. - GPU-only timing breakdown (excluding MSA). The problem.md requested this as a secondary metric. - True lDDT via OpenStructure for a representative subset (rather than proxy pLDDT). - Per-modality lDDT (protein vs ligand vs nucleic acid) for the winning configuration. **Tier assessment:** The ode-sampler orbit meets Tier 1 (methods paper) requirements for the main result. The negative orbits (compile-tf32, flash-sdpa, early-exit-recycling) collectively form a strong ablation study. Missing experiments before submission: CASP15 validation + GPU-only timing. [UPGRADE TO TIER 2] orbit/ode-sampler: Main result is ready for methods paper section. Specific improvements needed: - [ ] Validate on Tier 1 CASP15 set (not just 3 complexes) - [ ] Add GPU-only timing breakdown (subtract MSA latency) - [ ] Add true lDDT via OpenStructure on representative subset - [ ] Clean implementation (remove monkey-patch, add `--deterministic_sampling` CLI flag) --- ### Graduation Candidates | Orbit | Status | Metric | Why ready | |-------|--------|--------|-----------| | orbit/ode-sampler | winner, promising | 1.79x | Best result; fully explored at config level; clean log with novelty and prior art sections. Outstanding: needs-cleanup label present — resolve before graduating. | | orbit/step-reduction | promising | 1.73x | Well-documented; parent of 4 child orbits; pareto frontier figure exists. Outstanding: needs-cleanup label present. | No orbits ready for graduation yet — both candidates have outstanding `needs-cleanup` labels. Once those cleanup items are resolved, run `/graduate` to promote orbit/ode-sampler into main. --- ### Narrative Arc **Thesis:** Boltz-2 end-to-end inference latency is dominated by trunk recycling (MSA + Pairformer, 4 passes) and MSA server latency, not by the diffusion sampling loop. The highest-leverage inference-time optimization is eliminating trunk recycling (`recycling_steps=0`) combined with deterministic ODE sampling at 20 steps, which together yield 1.79x speedup at +1.96pp quality. The config-space ceiling with current evaluator architecture is ~1.8x; reaching 2x+ requires structural changes (MSA pre-caching, torch upgrade, bf16 trunk, or persistent model serving). **Key evidence:** - orbit/step-reduction (1.73x): Recycling reduction yields 1.7x; step reduction alone yields 0.95x. Trunk is the bottleneck. - orbit/ode-sampler (1.79x): ODE sampling adds 7% improvement on top; extends viable step count to 10 steps where SDE collapses. - orbit/compile-tf32 (0.97x, dead-end): TF32 is irrelevant for bf16-mixed models; torch.compile harmful in subprocess architecture. - orbit/flash-sdpa (0.745x, dead-end): FA2 blocked by pair bias; SDPA slower than einsum at Boltz sequence lengths. - orbit/early-exit-recycling (1.69x, dead-end): Adaptive exit requires 2 trunk passes minimum; dominated by recycling=0 (1 pass). - orbit/l40s-kernels (diagnostic): cuequivariance kernels blocked by cublas version conflict; bf16 triangular multiply shows 1.94x potential on isolated op; TF32 invisible in e2e. - orbit/combined-fast (1.66x, dead-end): Exhaustive config sweep confirms ceiling at 1.7–1.85x; identifies 4 architectural paths to 2x+. **Open questions:** - Does 1.79x hold on a 50+ complex Tier 1 (CASP15) evaluation? MSA variance on 3 complexes gives ~10% uncertainty in the speedup ratio. - Can removing `x.float()` from `triangular_mult.py:116` unlock bf16 throughout the trunk without quality regression? The isolated op shows 1.94x; the end-to-end gain is unknown. - Does torch 2.6+ with cuequivariance kernels provide measurable e2e speedup once MSA latency is controlled for? - Would persistent model serving (eliminate per-complex model load) add 5–10s of savings, pushing the ceiling to ~2x even without MSA pre-caching? - Is the pLDDT improvement at recycling=0 real (trunk over-recycling hurts quality?) or an artifact of the 3-complex test set? **Story arc (if writing a paper today):** 1. Introduction: Boltz-2 pipeline latency bottleneck is misidentified in the literature as the diffusion loop; we show it is the trunk recycling + MSA combination. Motivation: campaign-scale binder design where hundreds of predictions are needed. 2. Method: Systematic ablation of all inference-time optimizations: step reduction, ODE sampling, recycling elimination, precision/compile, attention kernels. Our approach: recycling=0 + ODE-20 as the Pareto-optimal configuration. 3. Results: 1.79x speedup at +1.96pp quality (iso-quality constraint satisfied with margin). Qualitative shift: ODE extends viable step range from 20 to 10. 4. Analysis: Negative controls (TF32, compile, SDPA) as ablation ruling out diffusion-loop approaches. Time breakdown showing diffusion is 7–12% of optimized runtime. 5. Missing: CASP15 validation, GPU-only timing, per-modality lDDT, clean implementation. **Shift from Milestone 1 narrative:** Previous thesis was "trunk recycling is the bottleneck, ODE and kernel optimizations remain open." New thesis: "ODE-20+recycling=0 is the Pareto-optimal config-space solution at 1.79x; the 2x+ question is now an architectural/systems question, not a configuration question." The campaign should either conclude at 1.79x (sufficient for many production use cases) or pivot to a new class of interventions. --- ### Recommendations for Next Hypotheses (if continuing) **1. MSA pre-caching (highest priority for reaching 2x+)** Run the evaluator with pre-computed MSAs (`--msa_directory`) for all 3 test complexes. This eliminates the 5–30s MSA server latency that currently masks GPU-level improvements and inflates timing variance. With MSA cached, the evaluation becomes deterministic enough to measure 2–5s improvements. This is not a speed optimization itself — it is an infrastructure fix that enables the next wave of optimization orbits to see their actual impact. Estimated effort: 1 orbit to cache MSAs and re-run the existing best config (ODE-20/0r) to establish a clean GPU-only baseline. **2. bf16 triangular multiply (highest expected GPU speedup)** Remove the `x.float()` upcast in `triangular_mult.py:116` and run the Pairformer trunk in bf16 throughout. orbit/l40s-kernels measured 1.94x speedup on the isolated einsum. The trunk is ~5–10s of the 39s optimized runtime; a 1.9x speedup on trunk alone would save ~3–5s (pushing from 1.79x toward ~2x). Risk: quality regression from reduced precision in triangular multiplication. Mitigation: run the standard 3-run validation protocol; add per-complex pLDDT and per-complex lDDT. Prior art: orbit/flash-sdpa showed attention in bf16 is safe (<0.3pp regression); triangular multiply may behave differently due to accumulated sum structure. **3. Torch 2.6+ upgrade + cuequivariance kernels** Update the torch version from 2.5.1 to 2.6+, which ships with cublas 12.6+ and resolves the version conflict that blocks cuequivariance. The cuequivariance library provides fused CUDA kernels for exactly the operations that dominate Boltz trunk time: triangular attention and triangular multiplication. orbit/l40s-kernels documented the exact conflict (nvidia-cublas-cu12 version pinning). This requires a Modal environment rebuild but no code changes. Note: evaluate whether torch 2.6 introduces any compatibility issues with the existing boltz code (checkpoint loading, bf16 behavior, etc.) before committing to a full orbit. --- ### Explore/Refine Triage ``` ### Action Mix CONCLUDE orbit/compile-tf32: TF32 is structurally irrelevant for bf16-mixed models; torch.compile is disabled by Boltz at inference time; no configuration change can overcome these. Fully explored. CONCLUDE orbit/flash-sdpa: FA2 is architecturally blocked by pair bias; SDPA slower than einsum at Boltz sequence lengths; no path forward without model retraining. Fully explored. CONCLUDE orbit/early-exit-recycling: 2-trunk-pass minimum cost is a structural constraint; no threshold tuning changes this. Fully explored. CONCLUDE orbit/combined-fast: Exhaustive config sweep; explicitly confirms no config-only path beyond 1.8x. Fully explored. CONCLUDE orbit/l40s-kernels: Diagnostic complete; cuequivariance blocked by torch version; findings documented. Fully explored. REFINE orbit/step-reduction: Clean up outstanding `needs-cleanup` items; ensure pareto frontier figure updated to include ODE-sampler data point. Then mark as graduation candidate. REFINE orbit/ode-sampler: Resolve `needs-cleanup` label items. Add GPU-only timing measurement (pre-cache MSA for at least one run). Once clean, graduate into main. EXPLORE msa-cache: Pre-cache MSA for all 3 test complexes; re-establish clean GPU-only baseline without MSA server variance. This is the infrastructure prerequisite for all remaining optimization orbits. EXPLORE bf16-trunk: Remove x.float() upcast in triangular_mult.py; validate quality; measure e2e speedup on cached-MSA baseline. EXPLORE torch-upgrade: Update torch to 2.6+; rebuild Modal environment; test cuequivariance kernel compatibility; measure speedup on triangular attention/multiply. Search balance: explore=3 refine=2 conclude=5 (explore ratio: 33% — appropriate given the config ceiling has been reached and new approaches need infrastructure support) ``` --- *Milestone 2 review complete — 7 orbits surveyed, 1 winner (ode-sampler at 1.79x), 5 dead-ends concluded, 3 new hypotheses recommended targeting the 2x+ threshold via architectural interventions.*

## IMPORTANT: Metric Recalibration

**orbit/msa-cache-baseline complete**: Established clean GPU-only baseline with cached MSAs.

**Links:** [branch](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/tree/orbit/msa-cache-baseline) · [log](https://github.com/canele-ai/boltz/blob/orbit/msa-cache-baseline/orbits/msa-cache-baseline/log.md)

### New Cached Baseline Numbers
| Config | Time (cached) | Time (MSA server) | pLDDT |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|-------|
| Baseline (200s/3r) | **55.96s** | 70.37s | 0.7046 |
| Winner (ODE-20/0r) | **37.45s** | 39.3s | 0.7303 |
| **GPU-only speedup** | **1.49x** | 1.79x | |

**The 1.79x was inflated.** The baseline spent 20.5% of wall time on MSA fetches (14.4s) while the winner spent only 4.7% (1.9s). With both using cached MSAs, the true GPU-only speedup is **1.49x**.

**The good news:** Timing variance dropped from ~10-15% CV to <1% CV. We can now see 2-3s improvements that were previously hidden. This makes GPU optimizations (cuequivariance kernels, bf16 trunk) measurable.

**Implication:** The 2x target requires finding ~18s of additional savings from 37.45s. That's a 50% reduction in the optimized path — significant but potentially achievable with cuequivariance kernels + bf16 trunk + further step reduction.

## Research Loop — New Exploration Wave (2026-04-12)

**Current best:** 1.48x speedup (eval-v2-winner, single-container) / 1.34x (cross-container mean)
**Concluded:** 12 prior orbits (5 dead-end, 4 promising, 3 graduated/infrastructure)

### New Orbits Launched

Based on codebase analysis identifying three major optimization barriers:

| Orbit | Issue | Direction | Key Insight |
|-------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **profile-and-fuse** | #15 | Profiling + Triton fusion | No profiling data exists for eval-v2 config — all optimization is guesswork |
| **compile-noguard** | #16 | torch.compile without guards | boltz2.py:459-481 explicitly UNDOES compilation at inference via `_orig_mod` fallback |
| **lightning-strip** | #17 | Lightning bypass + lower-level | Measure Lightning framework overhead; explore torch.export/TensorRT |

### Codebase Findings That Motivated These Directions

1. **Compilation is disabled at inference** — `self.pairformer_module._orig_mod` fallback (boltz2.py:478) means torch.compile does nothing during predict()
2. **`@torch.compiler.disable`** on `kernel_triangular_mult()` and `kernel_triangular_attn()` prevents fusion with surrounding ops
3. **Explicit `.float()` upcasts** in attention (attentionv2.py:101-108) force redundant memory traffic
4. **3 unfused rotation einsums** per diffusion step (diffusionv2.py:354-362)
5. **No profiling data** — we don't know the actual time breakdown on eval-v2

### User-Directed Exploration Angles
- Kernel-level optimizations (cuequivariance kernels enabled — what's left?)
- PyTorch Lightning abstraction overhead
- Going lower than PyTorch (Triton, torch.export, TensorRT)

timeline.text:
## 🏆 New Best: 1.55x from orbit/lightning-strip (#17)

**Previous best:** 1.48x (eval-v2-winner)
**New best:** 1.55x (lightning-strip) — quality gate PASS (pLDDT +0.44pp)

### Key Discovery
Lightning overhead itself is negligible (<100ms). The real bottleneck is **subprocess model loading** — each eval harness call spawns a fresh process that imports boltz (~2s), loads a 3GB checkpoint (~8s), and moves to GPU (~10s).

By loading the model once and running all test cases sequentially (bypassing both Lightning Trainer and subprocess-per-case):
- **GPU-only speedup: 7.62x** (this is the true computation speedup)
- **Including MSA: 4.24x** (amortized load, but MSA variance remains)
- **Eval metric: 1.55x** (amortized over 3 test cases including load)

For production pipelines running N>>3 predictions, speedup approaches **4.24x** asymptotically.

### Orbit Status Update
| Orbit | Issue | Metric | Status |
|-------|-------|--------|--------|
| **lightning-strip** | #17 | **1.55x** 🏆 | promising, winner |
| compile-noguard | #16 | 0.88x | dead-end (compile overhead dominates at 20 steps) |
| profile-and-fuse | #15 | — | still running |

timeline.text:
## Wave Complete: All 3 Orbits Finished

### Results Summary

| Orbit | Issue | Metric | Verdict | Key Finding |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|-------------|
| **lightning-strip** | #17 | **1.55x** 🏆 | promising | Model loading (~20s) is the real bottleneck, not Lightning dispatch |
| compile-noguard | #16 | 0.88x | dead-end | torch.compile works but 90-100s compilation overhead dominates at 20 steps |
| profile-and-fuse | #15 | 0.77x | dead-end | Kernel opts (SDPA, bf16 OPM) invisible under MSA variance; found TF32 override bug |

### Strategic Insights

1. **Model loading dominates**: The subprocess-per-case pattern wastes ~20s per complex on import + checkpoint load + GPU transfer. Lightning-strip's single-load approach reduces this dramatically (1.55x eval, 4.24x asymptotic).

2. **MSA variance masks GPU improvements**: Kernel-level optimizations (5-15% GPU time) are invisible when MSA server adds 30-70s variance. **Future kernel work MUST use pre-cached MSAs** to resolve signal from noise.

3. **torch.compile is closed for 20-step inference**: 90-100s compilation overhead cannot be amortized over 20 forward passes. Only viable for batch serving (compile once, run many).

4. **TF32 override bug discovered**: `boltz/main.py:predict()` line 1096 resets matmul_precision to "highest", potentially nullifying TF32 benefits in prior orbits.

### Recommended Next Directions
- **Refine lightning-strip**: Combine single-load approach with MSA caching for even larger gains
- **MSA caching + GPU timing isolation**: Use pre-cached MSAs to get clean GPU-only measurements, enabling kernel optimization work
- **Warm-start serving**: Persistent model server that accepts multiple predictions without reloading

## Orbit Updates (round 2)

Both `reduce-overhead` (+4.2s) and `default` (+5.8s) modes are slower than uncompiled baseline.

timeline.author: liambai2

timeline.text:
## Strategic Assessment After 5 Dead Ends

### What we've learned
Every approach that tries to make **existing computations faster** has failed:
- **SDPA attention** (#19): pair bias blocks FlashAttention-2
- **torch.compile** (#20, #24): cuequivariance kernels already minimize dispatch overhead  
- **CUDA graphs** (#24): dynamic shapes + model_cache mutation prevent capture
- **bf16 attention** (#23): TF32 fp32 einsum via cuBLAS is already faster than bf16 einsum
- **bf16 pairformer** (#23): destroys quality (-6pp)

### Root cause
**The GPU is already well-utilized.** cuequivariance kernels + TF32 + optimized cuBLAS paths mean there's little headroom in making existing operations faster. The current 1.34-1.47x speedup from eval-v2-winner comes from **doing less work** (20 steps instead of 200, 0 recycling instead of 3), not from faster computation.

### The only path forward: do even less work
To beat 1.47x, we need approaches that **reduce the total amount of GPU compute**:
1. **Layer pruning** — skip Pairformer or diffusion transformer layers (24→12?)
2. **Early exit** — stop diffusion when convergence is detected (<20 steps)
3. **Channel pruning** — reduce hidden dim from 768→384 for some layers
4. **Knowledge distillation** — train a smaller model (requires training infrastructure)
5. **Sparse attention** — skip distant token pairs in the attention computation

### Still running
- **triton-diffusion** (#22) — custom Triton attention kernel. May help if it reduces memory bandwidth pressure, but unlikely to beat cuBLAS+cuequivariance.
- **compile-score-v3** (#20) — likely dead end per cuda-graph findings.

### Next wave: 'do less work' orbits
